saida_bruta:
As the boys climbed the hill, she noticed them falter and confer. "You ask her, Niel."

"It's better if you do, George. She visits your house often, but hardly knows me."

Pausing at the three steps to the porch, Mrs. Forrester appeared at the door, nodding kindly with a pink rose in her hand.

"Good morning, boys. Heading out for a picnic?"

George Adams stepped forward, removing his wide straw hat with gravity. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and wade in the marsh and eat our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course. Enjoy your day. School's been out for a while now, hasn't it? Don't you miss it? I think Niel must. Judge Pommeroy says he's very diligent."

The boys chuckled, while Niel appeared uneasy.

"Off you go, and mind the gate to the pasture—don't leave it open. Mr. Forrester doesn't want the cattle in his blue grass."

They slipped quietly around the house to the grove gate, then dashed down the grassy slopes beneath the tall trees, calling to each other. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they vanished over the hill's crest. She turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, when you're baking this morning, make some cookies for those boys. I'll bring them down at lunchtime."

saida_bruta:
As the boys ascended the hill, she saw them hesitate and exchange words. "You ask her, Niel."

"You should do it, George. She's a regular at your place, but she barely knows me."

They stopped at the three porch steps as Mrs. Forrester opened the door, greeting them warmly with a pink rose. 

"Good morning, boys. Off to a picnic?"

George Adams moved ahead, solemnly lifting his straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and wade in the marsh and have our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course. Have fun. School's been out for some time, hasn't it? Don't you miss it? I imagine Niel might. Judge Pommeroy says he's very industrious."

The boys laughed softly, while Niel shifted uncomfortably.

"Go on now, just be sure to close the pasture gate behind you. Mr. Forrester doesn't want his blue grass trampled by cattle."

They slipped quietly around the house to the grove gate, then raced down the grassy slopes under the tall trees, calling out to each other. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they disappeared over the hilltop. She turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, when you bake this morning, make some cookies for those boys. I'll take them down at lunch."

As the boys climbed the hill, she noticed them pause and whisper to each other. "You ask her, Niel."

"It should be you, George. She comes to your place often, but she hardly knows me."

They halted at the bottom of the three porch steps just as Mrs. Forrester opened the door, holding out a pink rose in welcome.

"Good morning, boys. Headed on a picnic?"

George stepped forward, respectfully removing his straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. Could we fish in the marsh and eat our lunch in the grove?"

"Certainly. Enjoy yourselves. School’s been over a while now, hasn’t it? Don’t you miss it? I expect Niel might. Judge Pommeroy tells me he’s very diligent."

The boys chuckled quietly, while Niel shuffled his feet.

"Go along now, but remember to shut the pasture gate behind you. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want the cattle trampling his blue grass."

"Mary, when you’re baking this morning, make some cookies for those boys. I’ll bring them down at lunchtime."

saida_bruta:
As the boys ascended the hill, she saw them stop and exchange quiet words. "You ask her, Niel."

"It ought to be you, George. She’s always at your house, but I barely know her."

They paused at the foot of the three porch steps just as Mrs. Forrester appeared at the door, extending a pink rose in greeting.

"Good morning, boys. Off for a picnic?"

George stepped ahead, politely tipping his straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish in the marsh and have our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course. Have fun. School’s been out for some time now, hasn’t it? Don’t you miss it? I imagine Niel might. Judge Pommeroy says he’s very studious."

The boys suppressed a laugh as Niel fidgeted uncomfortably.

"Go on, but don’t forget to close the pasture gate after you. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want the cattle ruining his blue grass."

They tiptoed around the house to the grove gate, then raced down the leafy inclines under the towering trees, calling to one another. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester observed them until they disappeared over the hilltop. She turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you’re baking this morning, prepare some cookies for those boys. I’ll take them down at lunchtime."

saida_bruta:
As the boys ascended the hill, she observed them pause and exchange hushed words. "You ask her, Niel."

"She’s always at your house, George. I barely know her—it should be you."

They lingered at the foot of the three porch steps when Mrs. Forrester appeared, extending a pink rose as a greeting.

"Good morning, boys. Are you off for a picnic today?"

George stepped forward, tipping his straw hat respectfully. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish in the marsh and have lunch in the grove?"

"Of course. Have a wonderful time. School’s been out for some time now, hasn’t it? Do you miss it? I imagine Niel might. Judge Pommeroy says he’s quite industrious."

Trying not to laugh, the boys glanced at Niel, who fidgeted awkwardly.

"Have fun, but don’t forget to secure the pasture gate afterward. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want his blue grass ruined by the cattle."

They slipped around the house to the grove gate, then hurried down the cool, wooded slopes, shouting as they ran. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they disappeared over the hilltop. She then spoke to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you’re baking this morning, please make some cookies for those boys. I’ll take them down to the grove at lunch."

saida_bruta:
As the boys climbed the hill, she noticed them stop and whisper to each other. “You talk to her, Niel.”

“She’s always at your place, George. I hardly know her—you should do it.”

They hesitated near the bottom of the three porch steps as Mrs. Forrester emerged, offering a pink rose in welcome.

“Good morning, boys. Are you headed out for a picnic?”

George moved ahead, tipping his straw hat politely. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish in the marsh and eat lunch in the grove?”

“Certainly. Enjoy yourselves. School’s been out for a while now, hasn’t it? Do you miss it? I’d think Niel might. Judge Pommeroy tells me he’s very diligent.”

Suppressing grins, the boys looked at Niel, who shifted uncomfortably.

“Have a good time, but remember to close the pasture gate after you’re done. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want his blue grass trampled by cattle.”

They skirted the house and entered through the grove gate, then dashed down the shaded hillside, laughing as they went. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they vanished over the crest. Turning to her Bohemian cook, she said,

“Mary, while you’re baking this morning, would you make some cookies for those boys? I’ll bring them to the grove at lunchtime.”

saida_bruta:
As the boys ascended the hill, she saw them pause and exchange hushed words. “You speak to her, Niel.”

“She’s always at your house, George. I barely know her—you should go.”

They lingered near the base of the three porch steps as Mrs. Forrester appeared, extending a pink rose in greeting.

“Good morning, boys. Off for a picnic today?”

George stepped forward, tipping his straw hat respectfully. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish in the marsh and have lunch in the grove?”

“Of course. Have fun. School’s been over for some time now, hasn’t it? Do you find yourselves missing it? I imagine Niel might. Judge Pommeroy says he’s quite industrious.”

Suppressing smirks, the others glanced at Niel, who shifted uneasily.

“Enjoy yourselves, but be sure to shut the pasture gate when you’re finished. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want his blue grass ruined by the cattle.”

They walked around the house and slipped through the grove gate, then raced down the shaded slope, laughter echoing. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester observed until they disappeared over the hilltop. Turning to her Bohemian cook, she asked,

“Mary, while you’re baking this morning, could you prepare some cookies for those boys? I’ll take them to the grove at lunch.”

saida_bruta:
As the boys climbed the hill, she noticed them stop and whisper to each other. “You talk to her, Niel.”

“She’s always at your place, George. I hardly know her—why don’t you do it?”

They hesitated by the bottom step as Mrs. Forrester emerged, holding out a pink rose in welcome.

“Good morning, boys. Picnicking today?”

George approached and tipped his straw hat politely. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish in the marsh and eat lunch in the grove?”

“Certainly. Have a wonderful time. School’s been out a while now, hasn’t it? Do you miss it? I’d guess Niel might. Judge Pommeroy says he’s very diligent.”

The others glanced at Niel, stifling grins as he fidgeted.

“Have fun, but remember to close the pasture gate afterward. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want the cattle trampling his blue grass.”

They circled the house and passed through the grove gate, then dashed down the shaded hillside, their laughter ringing out. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they vanished over the crest. Turning to her Bohemian cook, she requested,

“Mary, while you’re baking this morning, could you make some cookies for those boys? I’ll bring them to the grove at lunchtime.”

They walked around the house, slipped through the grove’s gate, and hurried down the shady slope, their laughter trailing behind. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they vanished over the crest. She turned to her Bohemian cook and said,

“Mary, as you bake this morning, could you make some cookies for those boys? I’ll bring them to the grove at lunch.”
saida_filtrada:
As the boys climbed the hill, she watched them stop and murmur to each other. “You talk to her, Niel.”

“But she’s always near your place, George. I hardly know her—why don’t you ask?”

They hesitated by the bottom steps as Mrs. Forrester stepped out, offering a pink rose in welcome.

“Good morning, boys. Off for a picnic today?”

George stepped up, tipping his straw hat politely. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish in the marsh and eat lunch in the grove?”

“Certainly. Enjoy yourselves. School let out a few weeks ago, didn’t it? Do you miss it? I imagine Niel might. Judge Pommeroy says he’s very diligent.”

The others exchanged knowing looks while Niel fidgeted.

“Have a good time, but remember to close the pasture gate after you’re finished. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want the cattle trampling his blue grass.”

They walked around the house, slipped through the grove’s gate, and hurried down the shady slope, their laughter trailing behind. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they vanished over the crest. She turned to her Bohemian cook and said,

“Mary, as you bake this morning, could you make some cookies for those boys? I’ll bring them to the grove at lunch.”

saida_bruta:
As the boys ascended the hill, she observed them pause and whisper to one another. “You talk to her, Niel.”

“But she’s always near your house, George. I barely know her—why don’t you ask?”

They lingered by the foot of the steps as Mrs. Forrester appeared, extending a pink rose in greeting.

“Good morning, boys. Heading out for a picnic?”

George moved forward, touching the brim of his straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish in the marsh and have lunch in the grove?”

“Of course. Have fun. School’s been out for a few weeks now, hasn’t it? Do you miss it? I’d think Niel might. Judge Pommeroy tells me he’s quite industrious.”

The others exchanged glances while Niel shifted uncomfortably.

“Enjoy your day, but don’t forget to latch the pasture gate when you leave. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want the cattle ruining his blue grass.”

They skirted the house, passed through the grove’s gate, and hurried down the shaded incline, their laughter echoing behind them. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they disappeared over the hilltop. Turning to her Bohemian cook, she said,

“Mary, while you’re baking this morning, could you prepare some cookies for those boys? I’ll take them to the grove at lunchtime.”
